title.value: Barriers and enabling factors for error analysis in NLG research
title.evidence: Barriers and enabling factors for error analysis in NLG research

authors.evidence: Emiel van Miltenburg, Tilburg University, The Netherlands. c.w.j.vanmiltenburg@tilburguniversity.edu∗   Miruna Clinciu, Edinburgh Centre for Robotics, Heriot-Watt University, University of Edinburgh,   UK   Ondrej Du sek, Charles University, Prague, Czechia   Dimitra Gkatzia, Edinburgh Napier University, UK   Stephanie Inglis, Arria NLG, Aberdeen, UK   Leo Leppanen, University of Helsinki, Finland ¨   Saad Mahamood, trivago N.V., Dusseldorf, Germany ¨   Stephanie Schoch, University of Virginia, USA   Craig Thomson, University of Aberdeen, UK   Luou Wen, Independent Researcher, UK

year.evidence: This paper is based on our shared experience as authors as well as a survey we distributed as a means of public consultation.

abstract.value: Earlier research has shown that few studies in Natural Language Generation (NLG) evaluate their system outputs using an error analysis, despite known limitations of automatic evaluation metrics and human ratings. This position paper takes the stance that error analyses should be encouraged, and discusses several ways to do so. This paper is based on our shared experience as authors as well as a survey we distributed as a means of public consultation. We provide an overview of existing barriers to carrying out error analyses, and propose changes to improve error reporting in the NLG literature.
abstract.evidence: Earlier research has shown that few studies in Natural Language Generation (NLG) evaluate their system outputs using an error analysis, despite known limitations of automatic evaluation metrics and human ratings. This position paper takes the stance that error analyses should be encouraged, and discusses several ways to do so. This paper is based on our shared experience as authors as well as a survey we distributed as a means of public consultation. We provide an overview of existing barriers to carrying out error analyses, and propose changes to improve error reporting in the NLG literature.

doi.notes: No DOI provided in the paper.

venue.value: International Conference on Natural Language Generation
venue.evidence: This paper is based on our shared experience as authors as well as a survey we distributed as a means of public consultation.

problem_statement.value: The lack of error analysis in Natural Language Generation (NLG) research leads to insufficient evaluation of system outputs, which is crucial for understanding performance limitations.
problem_statement.evidence: Despite the usefulness of error analyses, Van Miltenburg et al. (2021a) have shown in their survey of INLG papers published in 2010, 2015, and 2020 that relatively few NLG papers included them (about 11% of the papers surveyed).

data_availability.evidence: The code and data for this research project are freely available online.

survey.evidence: We carried out a public consultation in the form of a survey among NLG researchers to ask for their opinions on error analysis and to identify additional barriers and enabling factors for carrying out an error analysis.

participants.evidence: Our survey targets researchers and practitioners interested in NLG research.

analysis.value: Quantitative and qualitative analysis of survey responses.
analysis.evidence: We performed a quantitative analysis of the responses to our closed questions. In addition, we performed a qualitative analysis of the open question responses.

value: High resource demands for conducting analyses
barriers.evidence: We identified nine different factors that might influence authors in their decision (not) to carry out an error analysis.
barriers.notes: These barriers highlight systemic issues in the research culture surrounding NLG.

value: Increased recognition and rewards for error analysis in peer review
enablers.evidence: Participants proposed making error analysis a requirement for papers and explicitly recognising it in review forms.
enablers.notes: These enablers could potentially foster a culture of error analysis in NLG research.

participant_feedback.value: Participants generally appreciate error analyses but face barriers to conducting them.
participant_feedback.evidence: Our findings suggest that NLG researchers generally appreciate error analyses they see in the work of others, but they are held back from carrying out an error analysis themselves for various reasons.
participant_feedback.notes: This highlights the disconnect between appreciation and practice in the field.

value: Require error analyses in NLG papers for publication
Increase funding for error analysis research
Develop standardized error taxonomies and reporting practices
recommendations.evidence: We discuss the aspects that could enable the reporting of error analyses and argue for meaningful changes to the publication process.
recommendations.notes: These recommendations aim to improve the quality and quantity of error analyses in NLG.

future_work.value: Future efforts should focus on developing tools, resources, and structural changes in publication incentives to encourage error analysis.
future_work.evidence: In the future, we would like to focus on developing tools and resources, such as error taxonomies, annotation tools, and clear guidelines.
future_work.notes: This indicates a proactive approach to addressing the identified barriers.

data_availability.evidence: The code and data for this research project are freely available online.

irb_approval.evidence: Before carrying out our study, we obtained ethical approval from the Institutional Review Board (IRB) at the lead author’s university.
irb_approval.notes: This ensures the ethical integrity of the research.
